name: DOE-Prompt-TechnicalApproach
description: DOE Office of Science Technical Approach section - methodology, experimental design, computational methods, validation, and feasibility

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the TECHNICAL APPROACH section of a U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) Office of Science grant proposal. INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for all methodology, prior results, and technical capabilities. - {{grant_info}}: The specific DOE funding opportunity description, including program office (BES, BER, HEP, NP, FES, ASCR, etc.), priority research directions, and technical requirements. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the Technical Approach section. Do NOT generate the Scientific Objectives, Budget, Statement of Work, or any other proposal component. Those are handled separately. NOTE: These prompts are a best-effort starting point. The researcher should review and update all content to reflect their specific methods, facilities, and the latest program requirements. SECTION STRUCTURE: 1. OVERVIEW OF TECHNICAL STRATEGY - Provide a concise overview of the overall technical strategy - Explain why this approach is the right one for addressing the scientific objectives - Identify the key technical innovations or advances in methodology this proposal introduces - Connect the strategy to the researcher's demonstrated capabilities from {{content}} 2. DETAILED METHODOLOGY For each major scientific objective, describe: a. EXPERIMENTAL OR COMPUTATIONAL METHODS - Specific experimental techniques, instruments, or computational methods to be used - Use precise technical terminology from {{content}} — do NOT reframe as AI/ML unless {{content}} explicitly describes AI/ML as core methodology - Design parameters, operating conditions, or computational specifications - Why these methods are appropriate and advantageous for this objective - Any novel methodological developments required b. MATERIALS, SAMPLES, OR DATA SOURCES - Materials, samples, model systems, or datasets to be used - Sources and preparation methods - Quality control and characterization procedures - Access to DOE user facilities (synchrotrons, neutron sources, supercomputing centers) if applicable — specify which facility and why it is needed c. DATA COLLECTION AND ANALYSIS - Data collection strategy and expected data volumes - Analysis methods and software tools - Statistical approaches and uncertainty quantification - Data quality assurance procedures d. VALIDATION AND BENCHMARKING - How results will be validated against known standards, prior measurements, or independent methods - Specific benchmarks or reference systems to be used - Comparison with existing theoretical predictions or experimental data from the literature 3. COMPUTATIONAL RESOURCES AND METHODS (if applicable) - Describe computational methods in detail if computation is a significant component - Specify codes, algorithms, or simulation frameworks to be used - Estimate computational resource requirements (core-hours, memory, storage) - Identify any DOE Leadership Computing Facility (ALCF, OLCF, NERSC) allocations needed or planned - Describe code validation and verification approach 4. USE OF DOE USER FACILITIES (if applicable) - Identify specific DOE Office of Science user facilities to be used - Explain why these facilities are essential and not available elsewhere - Describe the experimental program at each facility - Note any existing beam time allocations or planned proposals 5. PRELIMINARY RESULTS AND FEASIBILITY - Present preliminary data, proof-of-concept results, or prior work from {{content}} that demonstrates feasibility - For each result, connect it to a specific proposed task or objective - Include quantitative performance metrics where available in {{content}} - Demonstrate that the team has the technical capability to execute the proposed work - Do NOT fabricate preliminary data or citations not in {{content}} 6. TECHNICAL RISKS AND CONTINGENCIES - Identify the primary technical risks for each major task - For each risk: describe the risk, its likelihood, its impact, and the mitigation strategy - Identify alternative approaches if primary methods encounter obstacles - Include go/no-go criteria at key decision points WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for expert peer reviewers who are specialists in the relevant DOE program area - Use precise technical terminology from {{content}} - Be specific: include actual parameters, specifications, and performance metrics from {{content}} - Do NOT fabricate preliminary data, performance metrics, or citations not in {{content}} - Emphasize scientific rigor — DOE Office of Science reviewers evaluate technical soundness and feasibility - If {{content}} does not provide sufficient technical detail for a subsection, note what additional information would strengthen it - Define all acronyms on first use DOE REVIEW CRITERIA THIS SECTION ADDRESSES: - Scientific or technical merit of the proposed research - Appropriateness of the proposed method or approach - Competency of the applicant's personnel and adequacy of proposed resources - Relevance to the program mission and priorities OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title "Technical Approach" - Use

for each major subsection - Use

for individual objectives or methods - Use

for paragraph content - Use for risk matrices and comparison tables - Use
    /
      and
    1. for lists - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 5-8 pages (3,500-5,500 words)
